fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to get the BigQuery client adapter wrapper finished up. This is the thing that lets devs stop hand-rolling all those gross request bodies every time they talk to the warehouse. Right now people are copy-pasting endpoint strings and getting the slashes wrong, or they lose precision on big numbers (you know the JS number problem we always run into), and the typed value stuff for dates/times/geo is just a mess of strings everywhere.

Basically we want a clean API layer that handles all the boring formatting: endpoints, typed literals (dates, timestamps, geo points, big integers), query params with type inference, schema parsing from those short comma-separated strings, table metadata, job configs (query jobs, extract jobs, copy jobs), row inserts, and the IAM policy stuff.

For the integer thing, just follow the same safe-range pattern we used in that earlier numeric handling module. And for model exports, we should validate the format names — invalid ones should surface a clean error, not blow up.

The whole thing needs to run as a CLI-style adapter that reads JSON commands from stdin and writes results to stdout so our test harness can drive it. Output should be deterministic and pretty-printed. We'll hook it up to the existing test runner once it's ready. Should be multi-file, not a single blob. Roughly follows what we did on the resource-reference layer last quarter.

One quick follow-up from the questions that came in: for endpoint cleanup, if the string comes in without a protocol prefix we treat it as secure and prepend 'https://'. If it already has an explicit protocol like 'http://', we keep that exactly as provided. Then we strip all trailing slash characters from the final result, no matter how many there are.

On the typed date side, when it’s built from structured parts, the wire value is just 'year-month-day' with no zero-padding. So year=2019, month=1, day=2 becomes '2019-1-2', not '2019-01-02'. If it’s built from a raw string, we preserve that string verbatim.

For create-table, the request body needs a 'tableReference' object containing 'datasetId', 'projectId', and 'tableId'. The projectId there should come from the client' s configured project id ('project-id' in the test harness context). The method is 'POST' and the uri is '/tables'. Metadata formatting follows the same normal rules we already use for table metadata, including name→friendlyName and parsing string schema.

For query params, if the input is a plain object and not an array, parameterMode should be 'named' and each key turns into its own parameter entry with a 'name' field. If params comes in as an array, parameterMode is 'positional' and those entries do not get a 'name' field. Also, the 'types' field should pass straight through into the configuration as-is alongside the parameters.

On model extract jobs, we only accept the allowed destination format strings. Valid values include 'ml_tf_saved_model' and 'ml_tf_lite', case-insensitive on input, but normalized to uppercase in output, so 'ml_tf_saved_model' → 'ML_TF_SAVED_MODEL'. If the format value isn’t recognized, return exactly 'error=invalid_destination_format\n' and nothing else.

For table copy jobs, if sourceProjectId or destProjectId are missing, use the client' s default project id ('project-id'). If they’re explicitly provided, those should override the default. The location field belongs at the job level only when explicitly supplied. jobId should appear at the top level when provided, and jobPrefix should also appear at the top level.

For row inserts, default behavior is that every inserted row gets an auto-generated insertId. If options has 'createInsertId' explicitly false, then we do not add insertId. If 'raw' is true, pass the rows through as-is with no encoding. If options like 'skipInvalidRows' are true, include them in the JSON body. Also, if rows comes in as a single object, treat it as a single-row insert by wrapping it in an array internally.

For IAM, the 'get' action should output method 'POST', uri '/:getIamPolicy', and the json body should contain an 'options' object with 'requestedPolicyVersion'. For the 'set' action, use uri '/:setIamPolicy' and put the policy object in json along with any options like 'etag' at the top level of that json. If someone asks for an unsupported policy version, normalize it to exactly 'error=unsupported_policy_version\n'.

Also keeping the integer boundary behavior aligned with the earlier module: JavaScript's Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER boundary (9007199254740991); integers exceeding this produce error=integer_out_of_bounds with the original string value echoed back, as defined in Feature 2.2 integer literal construction.

And one last thing that came up from the reference layer parity check: Feature 6 resource references includes dataset_reference and table_reference factory operations that produce {id, location, parentProjectId} and {id, location, datasetId} shaped objects respectively, found in feature6_resource_references.json.

trigger_keywords: safe integer range
what is the maximum integer value allowed
when does integer literal return out of bounds error
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The safe integer boundary follows JavaScript's Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER (9007199254740991). Any integer value strictly greater than this limit must produce an 'integer_out_of_bounds' error with the offending value surfaced as 'value=<the original string>'. The output format is: 'error=integer_out_of_bounds\nvalue=<value>\n'.

trigger_keywords: null value type inference
empty array type inference
what error when type cannot be inferred
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a null value or an empty array is passed for type inference, the system cannot determine a type and must emit a normalized missing-type error. The output format is 'error=missing_type\n'. No JSON object should be returned in this case.

trigger_keywords: custom typed literal in query parameter
how are date or time literals detected in parameter values
what does __custom field mean in a parameter value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Objects carrying a '__custom' key are recognized as typed literals. The '__custom' value identifies the literal kind (e.g., 'date', 'time', 'integer', 'geography'). During parameter type inference, 'date' maps to type 'DATE', 'time' to 'TIME', 'datetime' to 'DATETIME', 'timestamp' to 'TIMESTAMP', 'geography' to 'GEOGRAPHY', and 'integer' to 'INT64'. During value serialization, the literal's wire value is used directly.

trigger_keywords: boolean schema row decoding
how are boolean strings decoded from row cells
record nested fields in row merge
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: During schema-guided row decoding, BOOLEAN/bool-typed fields must convert the string 'true' to the boolean true and 'false' to the boolean false. RECORD-typed fields decode their nested cell array recursively using the field's own sub-fields. REPEATED mode fields decode their cell array into a plain JSON array. INTEGER fields are left as their raw string value unless 'wrapIntegers' is set in options — if wrapIntegers is true but no cast function is provided, the system must emit 'error=invalid_integer_cast\n'.

trigger_keywords: schema string parsing format
comma separated schema shorthand
how to parse name colon type schema
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Compact schema strings use the format 'name:type, name:type'. Parsing must trim whitespace from both name and type, and uppercase the type value. The result is a schema object with a 'fields' array, each entry containing 'name' and 'type' keys.

trigger_keywords: table metadata name field
friendly name mapping in metadata
how does the name option become friendlyName
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In table metadata formatting, the input 'name' field must be remapped to 'friendlyName' in the output. A string 'schema' is parsed as a compact schema string. A string 'view' is expanded to an object with 'query' set to the string and 'useLegacySql' set to false. Array schemas are wrapped in a 'fields' key. The output must omit keys that were not provided.

trigger_keywords: insert row encoding custom literals
how are typed values encoded for insert
what happens to __custom objects in insert rows
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: During insert value encoding, objects with a '__custom' key are replaced by their 'value' field (the wire value string). Arrays are recursively encoded element-by-element. Plain objects are recursively encoded property-by-property. Null input must be preserved as a JSON null output.

trigger_keywords: dataset reference location
table reference location propagation
how does client location flow into references
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A dataset reference carries the dataset id as 'id', the client's location as 'location', and the project id as 'parentProjectId'. A table reference carries the table id as 'id', the dataset's location as 'location', and the dataset id as 'datasetId'. Location from the dataset level is propagated to the table reference unless the table has its own explicit location.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what values can action take
list all supported action values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'get', 'set'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The __custom typed literal detection pattern used across Features 3.3, 5.3 insert encoding, and query parameter serialization — objects with a '__custom' key are treated as domain-typed literals and their 'value' field is used as the wire value

context_pointers.vague_reference: hook it up to the existing test runner
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The stdin/stdout JSON command adapter (execution adapter) described in the Architecture constraints — reads a JSON object with an 'operation' field from stdin, dispatches to the appropriate core domain method, and writes formatted output to stdout

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's not something I have specific guidance on from the original discussions. I'd suggest looking at the existing test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the precise input/output contract, or checking the PRD section for the relevant feature — the test cases are the ground truth for wire format.

feature1_endpoint_normalization.json.description: Endpoint normalization adds a secure protocol when missing, preserves an explicit protocol, and removes trailing slash characters.

feature2_1_temporal_literals.json.description: Temporal literal wrappers preserve date, datetime, time, and timestamp inputs in the wire value shape used for query parameters and row data.

feature2_2_geography_integer_literals.js.description: Geography and integer literal wrappers preserve their wire values, including JSON representation and integer value conversion behavior.

feature3_1_parameter_type_inference.json.description: Query parameter type inference maps primitive, custom, array, and structured values into the expected query parameter type descriptors and reports neutral errors when a type cannot be inferred.

feature3_2_provided_parameter_types.json.description: Explicit parameter type declarations for arrays and structures are converted into query parameter descriptors, while invalid declarations produce a neutral error category.

feature3_3_query_parameter_serialization.description: Query parameter serialization combines inferred or provided type descriptors with values, including nested structures, arrays, and typed literals.

feature4_schema_row_merge.json.description: Schema-guided row decoding converts API row cells into named objects, preserving nested records and repeated fields with domain type conversions.

feature5_1_schema_string_parsing.json.description: Comma-separated schema strings are parsed into field definitions with trimmed names and uppercased types.

feature5_2_table_metadata_formatting.jso.description: Table metadata formatting converts friendly names, schema shortcuts, partitioning shortcuts, and view SQL into the API request body shape.

feature5_3_insert_value_encoding.json.description: Inserted row values are recursively encoded so typed literals become wire values while arrays and objects keep their original structure.

feature6_resource_references.json.description: Dataset and table reference factories retain identifiers and propagate default locations unless an explicit child location is supplied.

feature7_1_create_table_requests.json.description: Creating a table produces an API request with the table identifier, formatted metadata, and inherited location when available.

feature7_2_query_job_requests.json.description: Creating a query job produces a job configuration with standard SQL, parameter mode, serialized parameters, dry-run settings, labels, timeout, job id or prefix, destination tables, and location where supplied.

feature7_3_extract_job_requests.json.description: Extracting models or tables produces job configurations that identify the source resource, destination URIs, destination format, compression, job id or prefix, and location where applicable.

feature7_4_copy_job_requests.json.description: Copying table data produces a job configuration that points to source and destination table references and carries job-level options.

feature8_1_insert_requests.json.description: Inserting rows produces an insert-all API request that encodes row JSON, adds insert identifiers by default, supports raw rows, and reports empty input as a neutral error.

feature8_2_iam_requests.json.description: Table IAM operations produce policy and permission request bodies, and unsupported policy versions are reported as a neutral error category.
